title: There's a recurring theme in your writing you haven't named yet — writing-as-feeling-exit
html:
Question

"What's something you keep doing in your notes but have never named — that runs through 5+ summaries and is hiding in plain sight?"

The unnamed thing: writing is your exit mechanism, not your archive

You think you write to capture. You don't. You write to get out of a feeling. The act of writing itself is the technology — what gets written is almost incidental. This pattern shows up cleanly across at least seven notes spanning seven years, and you've never given it a name.

Look at the throughline:

The 2019 origin. 2019 09 10 low key writing this to end is literally titled with the mechanism: "I am low key writing this to end, more like slightly silence my thoughts or feeling for the girl you meet, Alexandria Nagy." You stated the thesis in the title of the note. Writing-as-silencer. The note doesn't preserve Alexandria — it disposes of her. You knew, at 17, on a flight, that the way to handle a feeling was to write it onto a page so it would stay there and you could leave.

The 2022 procedural version. 2022 10 25 ahmad jamal stream is the "junk-drawer" specimen — and notice what gets stacked in there: jazz, chatgpt wedge ideas, OSU coursework, and an unsent apology text to jo, the valeria graduation guilt, the "Get a therapist" repeated three times. Tactical lists and unsent emotional letters in the same note, in the same voice. The ranked todo and the unsent text are doing the same job — getting the contents out of the head and into a place where they can be left alone.

The 2024 transition specimen. 2024 12 14 departing china — written on the flight out, the moment you most needed to not feel everything you were feeling. The note opens by acknowledging the sadness ("so fucking sad to leave behind this chapter") and ends with "That's a wrap to this chapter…" You wrote the chapter ending so you wouldn't have to keep living in it. The note IS the closure ritual.

The 2025 forensic version. 2025 03 01 peacock bar incident is the most extreme specimen — you wrote it twice. First in raw second-person letter-to-self mode (the standard exit pass), then again days later in clean third-person lawyer-grade prose. The first writing exited the feeling. The second writing exited the legal exposure. Same mechanism, two layers.

The 2026 strategic version. 2026 01 13 rankett com — the "tweet that fucked me up" note. Someone publicly staked the claim you were privately working in. You didn't journal about the panic. You wrote a self-prompt asking yourself to deep-think on whether the space still has merit. Writing was the way out of the panic into the next move. The 2-5am late night cluster (per the existing query) does the same thing — writing converts amorphous dread into specific questions you can act on tomorrow.

The shape of the pattern. Every one of these notes does three things in order: (1) names a feeling that is currently happening, (2) restates it in the third person or as a question to be answered, (3) ends with a directive — "advance," "wrap," "deep think," "FITFO," "make it out the mud." The directive is the exit. The note is the door.

This is materially different from the note as letter to self concept page, which describes the form. The form is the second-person voice. The function underneath the form is the one you've never named: writing-as-feeling-exit, or in your own register, writing as the door out of the room you're in.

Suggested concept page name: writing as exit — sister page to note as letter to self (which describes form) and late night writing patterns (which describes timing). Writing-as-exit describes the purpose. The page should cite all seven specimens above and note the directive-at-the-end signature.

You might want to think about this because…

If writing is how you exit feelings, then the volume of your writing isn't a measure of how much you're processing — it's a measure of how much you're trying to get away from. The 2025-09-23 horchata note and the 2026-01-13 tweet note were both written because the feeling underneath them needed somewhere to go. This means two things worth sitting with: (1) when you stop writing, it might not mean you're fine — it might mean you've found another exit (good or bad), and (2) the things you don't write down might be the ones you can't yet leave. The unnamed thing is more honest evidence than the captured one. Worth scanning your gaps, not just your archive.

Sources

  • 2019 09 10 low key writing this to end — the literal "writing to silence" thesis in the title
  • 2022 10 25 ahmad jamal stream — junk-drawer with unsent apology text + tactical todos in the same voice
  • 2024 12 14 departing china — written on the flight, "that's a wrap to this chapter"
  • 2024 11 28 leading thoughts about 6 month adventure — the manifesto-as-exit form, two weeks before the China departure
  • 2025 03 01 peacock bar incident — written twice, first to exit the feeling, then to exit the exposure
  • 2026 01 13 rankett com — "tweet that fucked me up" → self-prompt deep-think
  • 2025 09 23 how im scraping by — the horchata mad-at-self moment is itself an exit attempt
  • late night writing patterns — the daytime tactical / 2-5am strategic split is the same mechanism on different timescales
  • Concept (existing, form): note as letter to self
  • Concept (proposed, function): writing-as-exit
